g.data-narration: Welcome to OS Design and Implementation. We will explore design goals, mechanism-policy separation, and implementation choices.
Designing an OS is challenging engineering. The design is influenced by hardware, system type, and intended users.
g.text: • No single "correct" way to design an OS
• Design influenced by: Hardware choice, System type (batch/time-share/real-time)

g.data-narration: Requirements are vague like 'convenient' or 'efficient'. They can conflict, so no unique solution exists.
g.text: ⚠ Goals are VAGUE and can CONFLICT

g.data-narration: User goals define what users want: convenient to use, reliable, safe, fast, and non-intrusive.
System goals define what developers want: easy to design, flexible, maintainable, efficient, and secure.
The key principle: separate mechanism (HOW) from policy (WHAT). This makes OS flexible and maintainable.
Example: Timer interrupt is a mechanism that preempts CPU time. It provides the HOW of CPU preemption.
Policy examples: FCFS, Round Robin, Priority scheduling. These are decisions about which process runs.
Benefits: Flexibility to change policies without modifying mechanisms, modularity, and easier maintenance.
Examples across OS areas: CPU scheduling, memory management, file access, I/O, and synchronization all use this principle.
Early OS used assembly for maximum control and performance, but it's not portable and hard to maintain.
C provides portability, easier development, and maintainability. Modern compilers generate efficient code.
UNIX was rewritten from assembly to C in 1973 by Dennis Ritchie, making it portable to new hardware.
Modern OS use C for kernel (95%+) with small assembly parts for boot code, context switch, and interrupt handling.
Summary: User and system goals guide OS design. Mechanism-policy separation enables flexibility. C provides portability.
g.text: No unique solution - design depends on goals, workload, and constraints

g.data-narration: Exam tips: Know user vs system goals, mechanism-policy examples, UNIX C rewrite, and why separation enables flexibility.
g.text: 1. List 4-5 points each for User Goals vs System Goals
3. Why C over assembly? Portability, maintainability
4. UNIX 1973 rewrite - classic example of portability
5. Remember: Same mechanism can support different policies!

g.data-narration: Microkernel exemplifies separation: provides mechanisms (IPC, scheduling) while user-space servers implement policies.
